data.title: Trump administration recalls numerous career ambassadors
data.description:
The Trump administration is recalling a number of ambassadors to the U.S., a union representing foreign service officials and the State Department confirmed on Tuesday.

The Associated Press reported Monday that nearly 30 career diplomats in ambassadorial and other senior embassy posts were being recalled to Washington, D.C. Those ambassadors, which include ambassadors from Africa, Asia, Europe, the Middle East and Central and South America, will be returning to the U.S. for other assignments, if they accept them. 

A senior State Department official told The Hill Tuesday that the recalls are “a standard process in any administration.”

“An ambassador is a personal representative of the President, and it is the President’s right to ensure that he has individuals in these countries who advance the America First agenda,” the official added.

But John Dinkelman, the president of the American Foreign Service Association (AFSA), said the recalls amount to “nothing less than institutional sabotage” that will “undermine the United States’ role in the world.”

Nikki Gamer, the director of communications and outreach for the AFSA, told The Hill that those affected were notified “abruptly, typically by phone, with no explanation provided,” which is “highly irregular.” Gamer added that the union is working to confirm those recalled, and cannot independently verify lists reported by other outlets.

Dinkelman noted that such a move is unprecedented in the 101-year history of the modern foreign service. Career diplomats, unlike political appointees made by the president, have risen through the foreign service ranks and often serve across multiple administrations.

“Yes, ambassadors and senior leaders serve at the direction of the president,” Dinkelman said. “But carrying out removals abruptly, without explanation, process, or respect for professional norms crosses a clear line. 

“It destabilizes the institution, weakens U.S. credibility, disrupts relationships with allies, and sends a chilling message to career diplomats that expertise and service matter less than political loyalty.” 

White House spokeswoman Anna Kelly directed The Hill to the State Department.

Since President Trump returned to office, the State Department has undergone a number of changes at the direction of Secretary of State Marco Rubio, including the shuttering of the U.S. Agency of International Development (USAID). The State Department is now operating programs previously ran by USAID directly. 

Rubio told reporters last week that he is “proud of the changes we’ve made in foreign aid.”

data.title: Schumer calls on DOJ to release details on Epstein co-conspirators
data.description:
Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (N.Y.) on Tuesday called on the Justice Department to release more details about 10 possible co-conspirators associated with convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

Schumer, who is pushing the Senate to sue the Justice Department for not fulfilling a Dec. 19 deadline to publicize all documents and records related to Epstein, says he wants more information about the 10 individuals that federal investigators considered Epstein’s possible co-conspirators.

“Buried in the Epstein files is an email disclosing the Department of Justice was looking into at least 10 possible Jeffrey Epstein co-conspirators. The Department of Justice needs to shed more light on who was on the list, how they were involved, and why they chose not to prosecute,” Schumer said in a statement.

“Protecting possible co-conspirators is not the transparency the American people and Congress are demanding,” he said.

Redacted emails released by the Justice Department reveal that federal agents tried to contact several individuals they viewed as potential co-conspirators after arresting Epstein in 2019 on sex trafficking charges.

Most of the names in the emails were redacted but three were not: Ghislaine Maxell, Epstein’s partner who was convicted of sex-trafficking in 2021; Leslie Wexner, a billionaire whose money Epstein helped manage; and Jean-Luc Brunel, a French modeling scout, who was suspected of scouting women for Epstein and later killed himself in jail.

Wexner cut off contact with Epstein after he was indicted in Florida for sex trafficking in 2007 and a lawyer for the business magnate told The New York Times that he was not a target of a federal investigation or viewed as a co-conspirator.

The Justice Department released 30,000 new documents related to Epstein overnight Tuesday.

They include emails between Maxell and a person known only as “A” that appeared to have a connection to the British royal family.

data.title: US adults are increasingly pessimistic about economy: Survey
data.description:
Confidence in the economy dipped to negative 33 in December, the lowest level recorded in President Trump’s second term, according to a new poll.

Gallup’s Economic Confidence Index — which reflects Americans’ views of current economic conditions and their future outlook — dropped 3 points from negative 30 in November, after falling from negative 23 in October. In August and September, the index held at negative 20, according to the data.

The latest results mark the economy’s worst showing since July 2024, when confidence stood at negative 35. The index was negative 19 when Trump took office in January and has hovered in the negative teens for most of his second term.

The index dipped to negative 20 in March and to negative 22 in April, before bouncing back the following month and peaking at negative 14 in June. The index has a theoretical range of negative 100 to positive 100.  

The decline in the index comes as 47 percent of respondents described the current economic condition as “poor,” up from 40 percent in November. Another 31 percent say conditions are “only fair,” and 21 percent say they’re “excellent” or “good,” the poll found.

More than two-thirds, 68 percent, say economic conditions are getting worse, and 29 percent say they’re getting better. Those numbers reflect increasing pessimistic in the American public, after 58 percent said in May that the economy was worsening while 37 percent thought it was improving, according to the pollster.

The survey comes as both parties renew their focus on affordability concerns following the 2025 elections and a record-long government shutdown. The president has taken steps to address rising prices, including rolling back some of his tariffs, while still at times dismissing “affordability” as a hoax pushed by Democrats.

Trump has struggled with falling approval ratings on his handling of the economy, even as his overall job performance numbers have seen a slight bump after falling to second-term lows in November.

The Gallup survey was conducted on Dec. 1-15 and included 1,016 adults. The margin of error is 4 percentage points.

data.title: Pulled ’60 Minutes’ segment airs in Canada, spreads online
data.description:
A CBS News segment focused on unearthing stories of Venezuelan men detained in a notorious El Salvador prison after being deported by the Trump administration has sparked controversy among viewers after it was blocked from airing in the United States but was showcased in Canada and online. 

CBS News editor in chief Bari Weiss pulled “Inside CECOT” from running on “60 Minutes,” explaining that the story was not “ready.” But correspondent Sharyn Alfonsi alleged the decision was not an editorial decision but instead “political.”

“Holding stories that aren’t ready for whatever reason — that they lack sufficient context, say, or that they are missing critical voices — happens every day in every newsroom. I look forward to airing this important piece when it’s ready,” Weiss said in a statement.

While the recently named editor in chief prevented the report on the overseas mega-prison from airing nationally, the episode briefly appeared on Global TV’s free website and app on Monday and remained available for two hours until it was removed, according to the Canadian Broadcasting Corporation. 

It was shared among social media users online. 

CBS News did not immediately respond to requests for comment about why the report was aired in the Great White North but restricted in the United States.

“Our story was screened five times and cleared by both CBS attorneys and Standards and Practices,” Alfonsi wrote in an internal email reported by NBC News.

“It is factually correct. In my view, pulling it now, after every rigorous internal check has been met, is not an editorial decision, it is a political one,” she said.

In an editorial call Monday morning, Weiss said that she “held a ’60 Minutes’ story because it was not ready,” a source told the outlet.

Weiss joined CBS News earlier this year after Paramount Skydance acquired her publication, The Free Press. 

Prior to ascending to the helm of CBS News, Weiss was known for leaving her job as a columnist at The New York Times after criticizing the outlet and others in the mainstream media for groupthink and partisanship.

Weiss reportedly told staffers on Monday she held Alfonsi’s segment while pushing for the journalist to get the Trump administration “principals on the record and on camera,” per NBC News.

“While the story presented powerful testimony of torture at CECOT, it did not advance the ball — The Times and other outlets have previously done similar work,” she said, according to that source.

However, Alfonsi said she made numerous requests for comment to the Department of Homeland Security, which referred them to officials at CECOT, who reportedly never responded.

Some have argued the piece was focused on the hundreds of prisoner subjects and not the administration. 

“A free press isn’t free if stories get shelved just because the powerful won’t talk,” Rep. Ro Khanna (D-Calif.) said in a social media post of his own. “CBS pulling the CECOT story on Venezuelan deportees sent to El Salvador’s brutal prison erodes trust. We are losing trust that government and media serve us, not the elite.”  

Alfonsi said men described “torture, sexual and physical abuse inside CECOT, one of El Salvador’s harshest prisons, where they say they endured four months of hell” throughout the segment.

Philippe Bolopion, executive director at Human Rights Watch, said he supported the report airing for broader awareness after the organization published an 81-page report in November about abuses at the prison.

“We look forward to the segment airing,” Bolopion told CNN. “The evidence is clear regardless of what airs on 60 Minutes: the Trump administration disappeared these Venezuelan men to a mega prison in El Salvador where they were systematically tortured.”

data.title: Will college education turn around in 2026? Probably not.
data.description:
College students are heading home for the holidays after wrapping another semester of questionable academic value. Parents of these students continue to pay big tuition bills for Johnny and Susie to enjoy football tailgates and the social scene. Broader American society, however, increasingly doubts whether the high college costs and burdensome student loans are sensible investments. 

The decline of American higher education is now a given — and the descent is accelerating. Recent polling shows nearly two-thirds of registered voters say a four-year college degree is “not worth the cost.” That’s a steep 23 percent drop off from just 12 years ago.  

Other polling, from the Pew Research Center, indicates 70 percent of Americans believe higher education is “going in the wrong direction,” a 14 percent jump in just five years. 

Grade inflation is rampant at colleges as professors pander to students’ feelings while avoiding making the tough judgements that grades should reflect. Colleges have surpassed even Lake Wobegon’s standard that all the children are above average, with a disproportionate number of students earning grades of “excellent.” That’s equity in action. 

More and more college attendees now qualify for accommodations that allow extra time on tests and test-taking in low-distraction rooms. Surely some students need special academic services, but data indicate that number has tripled at some institutions in less than a decade, so something else must be going on here. And what happens if students can’t find employers who will allow for extra time at the office? 

Colleges have been accused in recent years of indoctrinating students instead of educating them. Studies confirm that concern, as colleges brainwash students as opposed to expanding their brains. Activist, trendy curriculums and intellectual monoculture have replaced robust inquiry and free expression. The search for truth that characterized Western education for generations has been largely “cancelled,” as bias response teams and doctrinaire faculty limit the range of acceptable ideas.  

One-sided instruction that stifles debate limits learning. Pompous faculty would benefit from the wisdom of John Stuart Mill, who wrote, “He who knows only his own side of the case knows little of that.” 

In spite of all of these concerns, college administrators have had the gall to keep jacking up tuition, well beyond the nation’s general inflation rates. So, parents are paying inflated prices to have infantilized students get indoctrinated while pursuing flimsy degrees lacking heft. It took a while, but Americans have finally figured out the grand scam that has become today’s higher education. 

College administrators and governing boards have only themselves to blame for this decline in public confidence. This is a problem of their own making because of repeated and willful failure to communicate effectively to the American marketplace.  

College administrators misread their intended audience, smugly believing they didn’t owe transparent explanations to the commoners regarding increasing costs or curriculum creep. Perhaps these stuffy elites knew well that trying to explain the unexplainable would more quickly expose the insidious and vacuous nature of the academy’s drift. 

At the same time that colleges were failing to communicate its intentions to the external public, campuses were restricting the flow of ideas and forcing ideological groupthink to the internal constituency, their students.  

Noted journalist Walter Lippmann once wrote, “Where all think alike, no one thinks very much.” That certainly describes the professoriate these days, which is highly politicized and can’t restrain itself from forcing its views into virtually every field of study. Search committees for new faculty hires guarantee that incoming members of faculty fraternity adhere to the established groupthink. The forcing of liberal perspectives throughout the college environment doesn’t actually liberate students; instead, it creates illiberal conditions in which free expression is suppressed. 

Academic leaders seem unwilling to do the introspection needed to turn things around. They blame away the public’s declining support by pointing at the Trump administration, economic conditions and the demographic changes among high school students. They’d blame the full moon if they could. 

The time has come for college administrators to take intellectually honest messaging initiatives to explain how their institutions fell into decline. They must also sincerely discuss steps they will take to reinstate intellectual vitality into their ossified institutions.  

It was once the dream of most American parents to send their children to college. They believed that a college degree would empower their kids to fulfilling and prosperous lives. That’s not happening today like it should. Colleges won’t win back public confidence until they return to coherent missions and an intellectual tradition of seeking truth, and proactively disseminate those messages to the nation. 

Jeffrey McCall is a professor of communication at DePauw University.

data.title: Fewer people would recommend MMR vaccine: Survey
data.description:
As measles cases continue to spread across the country, a new survey shows a “small but significant drop” in the proportion of the public that would recommend someone in their household get the measles, mumps and rubella (MMR) vaccine. 

According to the poll, released Monday from the Annenberg Public Policy Center (AAPC) at the University of Pennsylvania, about 86 percent of people would recommend that someone in their household who was eligible for the MMR vaccine get the shot, down from 90 percent in November 2024. 

The declines are similar for other vaccines, which “should be cause for concern,” said Ken Winneg, APPC’s managing director of survey research. 

The results comeas measles cases are higher than in any year since the disease was declared eradicated.  

If the outbreaks continue spreading at their current pace — primarily in South Carolina and along the Utah-Arizona border— the U.S. is likely to lose its status as a country where the disease is eliminated.  

Infectious disease specialists say losing measles elimination status is directly related to President Trump’s appointment of Robert F. Kennedy Jr. to lead the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) and the vaccine policies he’s pursued on behalf of the Trump administration. 

The latest survey found 75 percent of adults would recommend an eligible person be vaccinated against the human papillomavirus (HPV), down from 79 percent in November 2024.   Another 85 percent said they would recommend the polio vaccine, down from 88 percent last year.

A similar share of adults said the MMR vaccine is safe, a number that has remained the same in surveys conducted in 2024 and 2023. But that is significantly lower than how people perceived its safety in August 2022, when 88 percent considered the shot to be safe. 

In the current survey, 83 percent said that the MMR vaccine is effective, down from 87 percent in 2022. 

Three in four people also said they believe it is safer to get the MMR vaccine than to get the diseases it protects, the poll found.

The AAPC survey of 1,637 U.S. adults was conducted from Nov. 17-Dec. 1. The margin of sampling error is plus or minus 3.5 percentage points.

data.title: Live updates: New batch of Epstein documents mention Trump; US economy surges in third quarter
Most Americans donated to charity in 2025: Poll
data.description:
More than 6 in 10 U.S. adults have donated to charity in 2025, according to a new poll, with most Americans who plan to give doing so before the end of the year.

The Associated Press-NORC survey, released Monday, found 46 percent of respondents have already made charitable donations in 2025, while another 18 percent have donated and plan to do so again before the year ends. 

Only 6 percent of respondents said they have not donated yet but plan to do so before the end of the year, when many charitable groups make big fundraising appeals. Thirty percent of respondents said they don’t plan to make a donation in 2025.

The poll found the most common organizations that respondents sent money to were those that help others in need in the U.S., religious institutions, animal care organizations, disaster relief organizations and educational institutions. 

More than 4 in 10 respondents said they have given money to someone in need they know personally, donated to a charity when out at a store or provided non-financial support to individuals in their community. 

Adults with a college degree were on average more likely to donate than those without a college degree. 

The majority of respondents who have donated or plan to donate soon said federal funding cuts and the 43-day government shutdown did not impact their decisions. The record funding lapse, between October and November, resulted in federal agencies furloughing thousands of workers and ceasing certain services. 

At the direction of President Trump, the federal government this year has cut funding for local governments, clean energy and housing support, among other areas, moves that have faced legal challenges. The president’s 2026 budget also proposes massive cuts to education, and the tax and budget law he signed in July made several changes to eligibility criteria for the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Programs (SNAP).

Nearly 30 percent of respondents said the funding cuts and shutdown did impact their charitable giving decisions in 2025.

The Associated Press-NORC poll of 1,146 U.S. adults was conducted via online and phone interviews Dec. 4-8 and has a margin of error of 4 percent.

data.title: Trump credits tariffs for surprisingly robust economic growth
data.description:
President Trump on Tuesday touted the latest data showing a robust expansion of the U.S. economy, crediting tariffs for the unexpected growth.

“The TARIFFS are responsible for the GREAT USA Economic Numbers JUST ANNOUNCED…AND THEY WILL ONLY GET BETTER! Also, NO INFLATION & GREAT NATIONAL SECURITY. Pray for the U.S. Supreme Court!!!” the president wrote in a statement on Truth Social.

In the third quarter, the economy grew by 4.3 percent with an increase in exports and consumer spending, according to data released by the Commerce Department. The quarter’s gross domestic product — the output of goods and services — rose from its 3.8 percent growth rate in the April-June quarter, per the wire.

Chris Zaccarelli, chief investment officer for Northlight Asset Management, told the Associated Press, “there isn’t as much need to worry about a slowing economy,” if growth rates persist at this level.

However, the fate of new trade rates remain uncertain as the Supreme Court weighs the legality of the Trump administration’s sweeping tariffs. 

The high court must evaluate if the president has the authority to set foreign trade rates without consulting Congress under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act. Justices heard oral arguments in the case in November.

Dozens of companies have sued the White House requesting refunds for tariffs, citing unexpected business deficits, should the Trump administration lose the Supreme Court case.

Their concerns come as inflation rates remain steady and after the Federal Reserve cut interest rates earlier this month for its third cut of the year.

data.title: Stefanik exits New York governor’s race after getting jilted by Trump
data.description:
Elise Stefanik didn’t just drop out of New York’s governor’s race. She walked away from the entire political bet she’s been making for years.  

On paper, her exit came wrapped in familiar language. Family. Timing. Strategy. In a post on X, Stefanik pointed to a potentially bruising primary and wrote: “As we have seen in past elections, while we would have overwhelmingly won this primary, it is not an effective use of our time or your generous resources to spend the first half of next year in an unnecessary and protracted Republican primary, especially in a challenging state like New York.”  

That explanation is tidy. But the larger story is messier and much more revealing.  

Stefanik was already facing long odds. Democrats outnumber Republicans two to one in New York, and the state hasn’t elected a Republican governor in more than two decades. A midterm election year wasn’t likely to help. But structural math isn’t what ended this campaign. Power dynamics did.  

For years, Stefanik built her political identity around proximity to Donald Trump. She reshaped herself, branded herself “ultra MAGA,” and played the loyalist role with discipline. That loyalty, she believed, would pay off.  

It didn’t.  

After Trump yanked her nomination to serve as U.N. ambassador, publicly undercut her, and then declined to endorse her for governor, the message was unmistakable. And it landed hard.  

In a long post on X she wrote, “My most important title is Mom.”    

That line matters — not because it’s insincere, but because it became the soft landing after a series of very public political blows. Donors were left frustrated. Allies confused. And Stefanik herself appeared, for the first time, politically untethered.  

To critics, this moment felt like consequence. Rep. Don Beyer, Democrat of Virginia, put it bluntly to The New York Times a year ago: “My greatest disappointment is Elise Stefanik, who should know better. She went off the deep end.”  

What Stefanik’s fall really crystallizes is the ceiling of MAGA loyalty. Trump can elevate you overnight. He can also leave you stranded just as fast. And he doesn’t explain himself either way.  

That reality became unavoidable during one of the most striking moments of this saga, involving New York City Mayor-elect Zohran Mamdani. Stefanik attempted to make Mamdani the face of the far left, even referring to him as a “jihadist.” But when Trump was asked to back her up, standing right beside Mamdani after their Oval Office meeting, he didn’t just refuse. He dismissed the attack and told a reporter: “You really have to ask her about that. I met with a man who is a very rational person.”  

That was political gut punch.  

Stefanik’s story isn’t just about one race or one endorsement. It’s about what happens when a political career is built around a single gravitational force. When that force moves on, you’re left scrambling to explain the wreckage.  

In Trump’s orbit, loyalty isn’t rewarded. It’s rented. And when the lease is up, you don’t even get a goodbye. 

Lindsey Granger is a NewsNation contributor and co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising.” This column is an edited transcription of her on-air commentary.
